headline: WEATHER FOCUS AS PLANTING WEEK BEGINS
subheadline: Corn holds $4.41 into critical planting window while cattle sit at historic highs.
lead: Markets enter the most weather-sensitive week of spring with corn at $4.41 and soybeans at $11.76 as of Friday's close. The next five days will determine whether Corn Belt farmers get their optimal planting window — or face the first delays that could reshape grain markets. Live cattle at $249.20 represent near-record territory that demands attention.
teaser: Critical planting week ahead with weather holding all the cards.

one_number.context: This is where corn sits heading into the most critical planting week of the year. Every day of delay past optimal dates costs roughly 1 bushel per acre, making weather forecasts more important than any USDA report right now.

sections.title: WEEK AHEAD GRAIN WATCH
sections.body: Corn at $4.41 and December corn at $4.72 head into planting's make-or-break week. Weather will drive everything — 7-day forecasts matter more than any fundamental analysis right now. Soybeans gained to $11.76 Friday but remain secondary to corn's planting urgency. The market's betting farmers get decent conditions, but April weather windows close fast.
sections.bottom_line: Weather forecasts will move markets more than any report this week.
sections.farmer_action: Watch 7-day forecasts obsessively and be ready to price any weather rallies — optimal planting windows don't wait.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK AT EXTREMES
sections.body: Live cattle closed Friday at $249.20, sitting at 98% of their 52-week range — essentially record territory. These margins won't last at these levels. Lean hogs eased to $103.72 but remain solidly profitable. Class III milk gained to $17.17, continuing its steady climb from winter lows. Historic cattle prices create both opportunity and risk.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle at historic highs need immediate pricing attention.
sections.farmer_action: Lock protection on remaining cattle immediately — margins this strong rarely sustain for long.

sections.title: INPUT COST PRESSURE
sections.body: Diesel proxy crude oil fell to $96.57, providing some relief heading into planting season. Natural gas at $2.65 sits at 52-week lows, keeping fertilizer costs manageable. Spring demand spikes are coming — current levels represent opportunity before seasonal pressures hit. Every farmer knows April through June drives the year's highest fuel consumption.
sections.bottom_line: Lock remaining fuel needs now before spring demand hits.
sections.farmer_action: Secure remaining diesel contracts this week — current levels are workable but vulnerable to demand spikes.

sections.title: MONDAY WATCH LIST
sections.body: Markets open with all eyes on weekend weather models and any overnight updates to 7-day forecasts. USDA export sales at 7:30 AM could provide direction, but weather trumps everything during planting season. Soybean meal's 3.6% Friday gain to $331.80 suggests protein demand remains strong. Dollar index at exactly $100.00 creates a clean technical level to watch.
sections.bottom_line: Weather forecasts will set Monday's tone before any economic data.
sections.farmer_action: Check weather updates first thing Monday morning — price any unfavorable forecast shifts immediately.

the_more_you_know.title: The Science Behind Optimal Planting Dates
the_more_you_know.body: Those 'optimal planting dates' aren't marketing hype — they're based on decades of yield data and soil temperature research. Corn planted before soil hits 50°F consistently struggles, but every day after the optimal window closes costs yield. University studies show 1 bushel per acre per day losses after optimal dates in northern areas. Weather isn't just about getting into the field — it's about soil conditions that determine the entire growing season.

daily_quote.text: The best fertilizer is the farmer's shadow.
